title: Broadcast Receivers
objectives: Understand BroadcastReceivers via a real-life analogy
Distinguish system, custom, and local broadcasts
Know the Android 8+ manifest-declared broadcast restrictions

sections.markdown:
**Think of a radio station.**

- The system (or your app) is the **radio station** broadcasting on specific frequencies (actions like `ACTION_BOOT_COMPLETED`, `ACTION_BATTERY_LOW`, or your own custom `"com.myapp.SYNC_DONE"`).
- A **BroadcastReceiver** is a **radio tuned to a specific frequency** — declared in the manifest or registered at runtime, waiting to catch messages. When a matching broadcast plays, the receiver's `onReceive` fires.
- Old Android was 'broadcast everywhere' — every app could hear everything. That was a battery-and-privacy nightmare: dozens of apps waking up on every `CONNECTIVITY_CHANGED` broadcast. Since Android 8, most **implicit** system broadcasts can no longer be declared in the manifest — they need runtime registration, which ties them to your app's lifecycle.

**Why they still matter:** system events (SMS received, boot, headphones plugged, package installed) still fire as broadcasts. Custom in-app messaging between components is still common. But the mental model has shifted from 'always-on radios' to 'radios that are on only when the app is alive.'

**A BroadcastReceiver** is an Android component that listens for **Intents broadcast across the system or app**. `onReceive(context, intent)` runs briefly on the main thread (< ~10 s or ANR). Two flavors: **manifest-declared** (system launches your app when the broadcast fires — heavily restricted since Android 8) and **context-registered / dynamic** (registered/unregistered at runtime, tied to a component's lifecycle).
## The two ways to register

rows: Rare system broadcasts still allowed (BOOT_COMPLETED, PACKAGE_ADDED for your own package); explicit intents
Anything tied to a component's lifetime — connectivity, screen on/off, custom in-app events
Use Flow / SharedFlow / EventBus alternative instead

sections.markdown: **The Android 8+ rule:** manifest-declared receivers for **most implicit system broadcasts** stopped working. Reason: too many apps had zombie receivers waking on every connectivity change. Exceptions: **explicit** broadcasts (someone sends to your specific receiver), plus a short whitelist including `BOOT_COMPLETED`, `LOCALE_CHANGED`, `TIMEZONE_CHANGED`, `MY_PACKAGE_REPLACED`, alarm actions, etc.
## Sample program — headphone plug listener + custom broadcast
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Doing heavy work in `onReceive`.** It runs on the main thread with a strict ~10 s window. Do minimal work; delegate to WorkManager for anything longer.
**Mistake 2: Forgetting `unregisterReceiver`.** Leaks the receiver, keeps the Activity in memory. Pair register in `onStart` / unregister in `onStop` (or `onResume` / `onPause`).
**Mistake 3: Manifest-declaring implicit broadcasts on Android 8+.** Won't fire. Register dynamically instead.
**Mistake 4: Missing `RECEIVER_EXPORTED` / `RECEIVER_NOT_EXPORTED` on Android 14+.** `registerReceiver` throws `SecurityException` without one. Use `NOT_EXPORTED` unless another app must reach you.
**Mistake 5: Using `LocalBroadcastManager` in new code.** Deprecated. Use a `SharedFlow`, `EventBus`, or DI-injected mediator instead.
## Best practices
**1. Prefer dynamic registration.** Ties receivers to the correct component's lifecycle; auto-freed on process death.
**2. Keep `onReceive` fast — under a second ideally.** Kick off longer work via WorkManager or a coroutine.
**3. Use `setPackage(packageName)` on internal broadcasts.** Limits delivery to your app — much safer.
**4. On Android 14+, specify `RECEIVER_NOT_EXPORTED` by default.** Only export when another app truly needs to send to you.
**5. Replace intra-app broadcasts with SharedFlow.** Type-safe, lifecycle-aware, coroutine-native.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Headphone plug listener.** Register/unregister with lifecycle; toast on plug/unplug.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Boot receiver + WorkManager.** On BOOT_COMPLETED, enqueue a one-off sync worker.
**Practice 3 (Hard) — Custom broadcast between two of your Activities.** Send a `"com.myapp.EVENT"` with `setPackage(packageName)`; receive in another Activity.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is a BroadcastReceiver?**

**A:** A BroadcastReceiver is one of the four Android app components. It listens for `Intent` broadcasts sent by the system or by apps. `onReceive(context, intent)` runs briefly (< ~10 s) on the main thread — do minimal work and delegate longer tasks to WorkManager. Two registration styles: **manifest** (declared in `AndroidManifest.xml`; system launches your app to deliver even if the app was dead — heavily restricted since Android 8) and **context-registered** (registered dynamically at runtime; tied to a component's lifecycle). Common uses: reacting to system events (boot, connectivity, headphone, SMS), and intra-app / inter-app messaging.

**Q2: What changed in Android 8 for BroadcastReceivers?**

**A:** Most **implicit** system broadcasts (broadcasts not targeted at a specific app) can no longer be declared in the manifest. Before Android 8, dozens of apps could each have a manifest receiver for `CONNECTIVITY_CHANGED`, and every network event would wake them all up — battery and performance disaster. Now, if you want to listen for such events, you must register at runtime — which naturally ties the receiver to your app being alive. **Exceptions:** explicit broadcasts (someone sending to your specific `ComponentName`), plus a whitelist including `BOOT_COMPLETED`, `LOCALE_CHANGED`, `TIMEZONE_CHANGED`, `MY_PACKAGE_REPLACED`, `NEW_OUTGOING_CALL`, and alarm-triggered actions.

**Q3: When should you use a BroadcastReceiver vs a SharedFlow vs LiveData?**

**A:** **BroadcastReceiver** — cross-app or cross-process events, and system-fired events (SMS, boot, connectivity). Only choice when the sender is another process. **SharedFlow** — intra-app events between components (VM → UI, service → observer) — type-safe, coroutine-native, no manifest ceremony. Modern replacement for `LocalBroadcastManager`. **LiveData / StateFlow** — for state (something with a current value observers care about). **Rule:** if the event crosses process boundaries → BroadcastReceiver. Otherwise → SharedFlow. Avoid BroadcastReceiver for intra-app messaging; it's heavier and less type-safe than the alternatives.

**Q4: Why must `onReceive` be fast?**

**A:** `onReceive` runs on the main thread and has a strict window (~10 seconds by default) before the system considers the receiver unresponsive and may kill the process with an ANR-style event. Additionally, if the receiver was launched by a broadcast to a manifest-declared receiver, the OS holds a wake lock while `onReceive` runs; long-running work drains battery and blocks other broadcasts. **Solution:** do minimal decision logic in `onReceive`, then delegate to WorkManager (`OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<...>()`) or a foreground service for anything substantial. For quick coroutine work, `goAsync()` extends the window a bit and lets you finish an async task before returning.

**Q5: Explicit vs implicit broadcast, and why does it matter?**

**A:** **Explicit** — the broadcast Intent names a specific `ComponentName` or target package (`setPackage(...)` / explicit `ComponentName`). Only that receiver gets it. Safer and less impactful on system performance. **Implicit** — the broadcast has an `action` string and no target; ANY receiver with a matching intent filter can receive it. That's why the system restricts implicit broadcasts starting Android 8. **Best practice:** for intra-app broadcasts, always call `intent.setPackage(packageName)` — makes an otherwise-implicit broadcast explicit, avoiding leakage to other apps and speeding up delivery.

## Real-world usage

- **System integrations** — SMS received, boot completed, package installed, battery low, headphone plug.
- **Push notifications** — FCM ultimately fires an Intent that a receiver handles.
- **Widgets** — RemoteViews click firing a broadcast the widget's receiver handles.
- **Media button events** — a receiver handles play/pause from Bluetooth headsets.
- **Alarm callbacks** — `AlarmManager` fires broadcasts at scheduled times.
- **Custom in-app broadcasts** — becoming rare; replaced by SharedFlow in modern apps.

## Advanced notes

- **`goAsync()`** — extends the receiver's window; call `PendingResult.finish()` when done.
- **Ordered broadcasts** — receivers can `abortBroadcast()` to prevent lower-priority receivers from receiving; use `<intent-filter android:priority="...">`.
- **Sticky broadcasts** — deprecated; used to keep the last broadcast for later receivers.
- **Runtime-registered receivers with a specific `Context`** — receivers registered with `applicationContext` outlive Activity destruction (leak risk); prefer Activity/Fragment context.
- **`RECEIVER_EXPORTED`/`RECEIVER_NOT_EXPORTED`** (Android 14+) — mandatory flag for dynamic registration.
- **Manifest-declared receiver + `<permission>`** — restrict who can broadcast to you.
- **`LocalBroadcastManager`** — deprecated, moved to unbundled `androidx.localbroadcastmanager` for legacy code only.

flashcards.back: Main thread; must finish in <~10 s or ANR.
Most implicit system broadcasts can no longer be manifest-declared.
Explicit names a target (setPackage/ComponentName); implicit is any matching filter.

quiz.q: Long work in a receiver should…
options: Enqueue WorkManager or start a foreground service

challenges.text: Register a headphone-plug receiver in onStart, unregister in onStop.
Manifest-declare BOOT_COMPLETED and enqueue a sync WorkManager job.
Send an internal custom broadcast with setPackage(packageName).
Replace an old LocalBroadcastManager pattern with a SharedFlow.
Use goAsync() to complete a small coroutine before returning from onReceive.

interviewQuestions: What changed in Android 8 for BroadcastReceivers?
When should you use a BroadcastReceiver vs a SharedFlow vs LiveData?
Explicit vs implicit broadcast, and why does it matter?
